DNA (deoxyribonucleic acid) is the genetic material of all living organisms. It carries the instructions needed to build and maintain an organism. DNA is made up of four different types of building blocks called nucleotides. Each nucleotide contains a sugar (deoxyribose), a phosphate group, and one of four nitrogenous bases: adenine (A), guanine (G), cytosine (C), or thymine (T).

The order of these nitrogenous bases along the DNA molecule determines the sequence of amino acids in proteins. Proteins are the primary functional and structural molecules of the body. They are responsible for carrying out many of the body’s functions, such as muscle contraction, the production of enzymes, and the transmission of nerve impulses. DNA sequences can be altered by a process called mutation, which results in a different protein being produced. Mutations are responsible for many genetic diseases.
